title: William Berg, 'Daphnis and Prometheus', TAPA 96 (

raw_text:
Eleanor Winsor Leach, 'Nature and Art in Vergil's Second Eclogue '
4 JP 87 (1966) 427-445.
After his previous review of Vergil's Epicureanism and of
Epicurean motifs in his poetry, Castelli (1) proceeds to a study of
hedone (otium) in the Eclogues , and of Epicurean expression and
and concept in the death and apotheosis of Daphnis {Eel. 5).
Notopoulos (2) has discovered that there is fifth century evidence
for scientific knowledge among the satyrs ( Oxy . Pap . 8 (1911)
1083, p. 60) and that Silenus' scientific expertise in Eel . 6 is not
a Vergilian novelty. Des Abbayes' verse translation of the Eclogues
is perfectly attuned to the original (3). Hornsby's 'new method'
text (4) provides, inter alia , criticism, questions and exercises
for Eclogues 1, 4, and 5. Berg (5) finds Prometheus as the forerunner of Daphnis in E. 5 . He suggests an Aeschylean inspiration
for the Eclogue , a satyr-play and the more serious tradition which
Aeschylus advances in the P. V. Daphnis, as devoted benefactor
of the pastoral world, finds his benefactor-model in the philanthropic Prometheus. Julius Caesar and his successor could also
be imagined in the same heroic dimension. Eleanor Leach (6)
assesses the second Eclogue as a paradigm for the entire collection, which favors the pattern of withdrawal and return in Corydon's
conduct. 'For him all pastoral is a deliberate experiment, and the
pastoral world is a temporary place of discovery and organization
for the poet and for man'.

Des Abbayes' verse translation of the Eclogues is perfectly attuned to the original (3). Hornsby's 'new method' text (4) provides, inter alia , criticism, questions and exercises for Eclogues 1, 4, and 5. Berg (5) finds Prometheus as the forerunner of Daphnis in E. 5 . raw_text:
Charles Segal, 'Orpheus and the Fourth Geòrgie: Virgil on Nature
and Civilization', AJP 87 (1966) 307-25.
Savage (7) examines the associations of Vergil's cognomen
Maro and suggests that in the course of the second Geòrgie Vergil
reflected on his own identity, linking himself with the historical
Maron at Thermopylae, the mythical Maron of Ismarus, whose wine
later subdued Polyphemus, and even (in the Culex) correlating the
77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:20:14 UTC
36
Gnat with a putative ancestor who roused the aged shepherd
(Hellas) to resist the monstrous serpent (Xerxes' host). Vergilius
is associated, by Savage, with the Greek Pleiades, as is his
mother Magia-Maia. Segal (8) finds different attitudes to nature in
both Aristaeus and Orpheus who 'frame' the essentially tragic life
of the bees. There is, he feels, a thematic contrast of man and the
bees, and there is also a stylistic contrast between the first and
second half of the book, between a Lucretian and a more Homeric,
more characteristically Vergilian half. And there is a more fundamental and revealing contrast between the 'real' world of bees and
the 'ideal' world of myth and poetry, which unlike the former, is
filled with death and disaster.

ТАРА 96 (1965) 321-336.
John Wright (9) discovers that Jackson Knight's estimate of A.
1, 461-2 is best satisfied by Richard Stanyhurst's Elizabethan
translation. Hermann, with characteristic ingenuity, argues that
Iopas is a pseudonym for Maecenas and that Iopas' song shows
traces of a Prometheus composed by his Augustan patron (10).
Hermann imagines the long-haired Iopas, with his intimations of
Lucretius and Maecenas, as a last homage to his generous benefactor. Rowell (12) argues that Servius derived all his information
about the Helen episode from Donatus' commentary which expressed no doubt about the authenticity of the verses. Donatus trusted
his sources on this matter and complied with their view. Stegen
(14) argues for the retention of vv. 567-88 on grounds of their harmony with the immediate and general ctm text. O'Brien and Twombly
(15) have composed a useful teaching manual for A. 1 and 2.
Jackson Knight's earlier and important studies entitled Vergil's
Troy: Essays on the Second Book of the Aeneid (Basil Blackwell,
1932) and Cumaean Gates: A Reference in the Sixth Aeneid to
Initiation Patterns (Basil Blackwell, 1936) have been combined in
a posthumously published collection with an essay from Vergilius ,
1939, "The Holy City of the East" (16). McKay (17) provides
topographical and historical commentary on the promontories of
Palinurus, Misenum and Caieta with up-to-date archaeological
accounts. Von Albrecht's (18) account of A. 6, 679-892 is a valuable review of earlier theories regarding the march-past and offers
new insights. J. J. Bray's (19) contribution to the Francis Letters
volume gives a full discussion of A. 6, 893-8, with critical attention to the views of Jackson Knight and Brooks Otis. Rutledge
(20) examines the role of the artist, thaumaturge, creative man and
benevolent ruler and finds that 'the artist Daedalus is a recurrent
figure, or motif, on various levels of signifie ance, throughout
Vergil's poetry', but the greatest of them all is Augustus. Zarker
(21) argues that the four panels of the temple doors at Cumae foreshadow and give greater significance to Theseus' adventures. An
epic figure from the past, he becomes Vergil's vehicle for a tacit

The article is filled with sensitive insights and commonsense.
Aeneas disappoints because 'he is cast in the mold of the Augustus from Prima Porta, not that of Hamlet: he suffers from his political responsibilities and his Augustan model'. The Marcellus passage is not merely Vergil's In Memoriam to the heir apparent but
'as an instance of intense grief in the present, he sums up for
Vergil and his audience the previous sufferings in Rome's past,
and points ahead to sufferings to come'. Caieta, for Segal, is the
replacement for the mother who cannot die, and her death completes Aeneas' experience with the loss of persons near and dear
to him: Palinurus, Misenus, Anchises and Caieta. Finally he explores the mythical and philosophical aspects of the Bough, which
belongs to both the upper and lower worlds and images the fundamental reality of man's position betwèen both. Stegen (23) reconstructs the prose redaction of A. 6, 595-655 and discovers two
main groups in the interior of Hades: (a) the enemies of Jupiter
and (b) other guilty parties (575-584; 595-627). He argues against
Havet's insertion of 616-20 between 601 and 602 and against the
substitution of quo for quo s at v. 602. De Grummond (24) finds a
fully realised figure of epic scope in Diomedes, a fighter become
philosopher-king, idealized yet warm, dignified, and courageous.
A new Diomedes has risen from the old likeness, regenerated in
his cherished Saturnia regna . Cannon (25) examines the characterization of Turnus, Pallas, Evander, Venus and Vulcan, and finds
them masterly and realistic. Duckworth's study of Nisus and
Euryalus (26) concludes that Vergil offers 'in dramatic form a
psychological study of two youths whose motives are basically
noble but who do the wrong things and suffer tragic deaths as a
result.' He argues that the story is neither an independent, unit
nor digression. He regards the story as preparation for the poet's
treatment of character and event in 9-12, and as of the utmost importance in foreshadowing the fate of Turnus. Lieberg (27) recognises the fundamental importance of Juno's role in the epic. The
motif of a Juno able to compromise probably derives from both
Naevius and Ennius, a character which Horace develops in Odes
III, 3. The reconciliation between Jupiter - Fate and Juno yields
a higher synthesis; and Juno enters the Roman pantheon as a
tutelary numen of Romula gens . Anne Booth's archaeological study
(28) draws on the Vergilian Venus 'for elucidation of the Ara Pacis

(21) argues that the four panels of the temple doors at Cumae fore-
shadow and give greater significance to Theseus' adventures. An
epic figure from the past, he becomes Vergil's vehicle for a tacit

The article is filled with sensitive insights and commonsense.
Aeneas disappoints because 'he is cast in the mold of the Augus-
tus from Prima Porta, not that of Hamlet: he suffers from his polit-
ical responsibilities and his Augustan model'. The Marcellus pass-
age is not merely Vergil's In Memoriam to the heir apparent but
'as an instance of intense grief in the present, he sums up for
Vergil and his audience the previous sufferings in Rome's past,
and points ahead to sufferings to come'. Caieta, for Segal, is the
replacement for the mother who cannot die, and her death com-
pletes Aeneas' experience with the loss of persons near and dear
to him: Palinurus, Misenus, Anchises and Caieta. Finally he ex-
plores the mythical and philosophical aspects of the Bough, which
belongs to both the upper and lower worlds and images the funda-
mental reality of man's position betwèen both. Stegen (23) recon-
structs the prose redaction of A. 6, 595-655 and discovers two
main groups in the interior of Hades: (a) the enemies of Jupiter
and (b) other guilty parties (575-584; 595-627). He argues against
Havet's insertion of 616-20 between 601 and 602 and against the
substitution of quo for quo s at v. 602. De Grummond (24) finds a
fully realised figure of epic scope in Diomedes, a fighter become
philosopher-king, idealized yet warm, dignified, and courageous.
A new Diomedes has risen from the old likeness, regenerated in
his cherished Saturnia regna . Cannon (25) examines the character-
ization of Turnus, Pallas, Evander, Venus and Vulcan, and finds
them masterly and realistic. Duckworth's study of Nisus and
Euryalus (26) concludes that Vergil offers 'in dramatic form a
psychological study of two youths whose motives are basically
noble but who do the wrong things and suffer tragic deaths as a
result.' He argues that the story is neither an independent, unit
nor digression. He regards the story as preparation for the poet's
treatment of character and event in 9-12, and as of the utmost im-
portance in foreshadowing the fate of Turnus. Lieberg (27) recog-
nises the fundamental importance of Juno's role in the epic. The
motif of a Juno able to compromise probably derives from both
Naevius and Ennius, a character which Horace develops in Odes
III, 3. The reconciliation between Jupiter - Fate and Juno yields
a higher synthesis; and Juno enters the Roman pantheon as a
tutelary numen of Romula gens . Anne Booth's archaeological study
(28) draws on the Vergilian Venus 'for elucidation of the Ara Pacis

authors: Failure in the Mission of Aeneas', ТАРА 96 (
title: ) 321-336. John Wright (9) discovers that Jackson Knight's estimate of A. 1, 461-2 is best satisfied by Richard Stanyhurst's Elizabethan translation. Hermann, with characteristic ingenuity, argues that Iopas is a pseudonym for Maecenas and that Iopas' song shows traces of a Prometheus composed by his Augustan patron (10). Hermann imagines the long-haired Iopas, with his intimations of Lucretius and Maecenas, as a last homage to his generous benefactor. Rowell (12) argues that Servius derived all his information about the Helen episode from Donatus' commentary which expressed no doubt about the authenticity of the verses. Donatus trusted his sources on this matter and complied with their view. Stegen (14) argues for the retention of vv. 567-88 on grounds of their harmony with the immediate and general ctm text. O'Brien and Twombly (15) have composed a useful teaching manual for A. 1 and 2. Jackson Knight's earlier and important studies entitled Vergil's Troy: Essays on the Second Book of the Aeneid (Basil Blackwell, 1932) and Cumaean Gates: A Reference in the Sixth Aeneid to Initiation Patterns (Basil Blackwell, 1936) have been combined in a posthumously published collection with an essay from Vergilius , 1939, "The Holy City of the East" (16). McKay (17) provides topographical and historical commentary on the promontories of Palinurus, Misenum and Caieta with up-to-date archaeological accounts. Von Albrecht's (18) account of A. 6, 679-892 is a valuable review of earlier theories regarding the march-past and offers new insights. J. J. Bray's (19) contribution to the Francis Letters volume gives a full discussion of A. 6, 893-8, with critical attention to the views of Jackson Knight and Brooks Otis. Rutledge (20) examines the role of the artist, thaumaturge, creative man and benevolent ruler and finds that 'the artist Daedalus is a recurrent figure, or motif, on various levels of signifie ance, throughout Vergil's poetry', but the greatest of them all is Augustus. Zarker (21) argues that the four panels of the temple doors at Cumae foreshadow and give greater significance to Theseus' adventures. An epic figure from the past, he becomes Vergil's vehicle for a tacit 77.43.89.117 on Mon, 27 Apr 2026 18:20:14 UTC 38 censure Athenia acterist nobler Segal's that cen and exp The article is filled with sensitive insights and commonsense. Aeneas disappoints because 'he is cast in the mold of the Augustus from Prima Porta, not that of Hamlet: he suffers from his political responsibilities and his Augustan model'. The Marcellus passage is not merely Vergil's In Memoriam to the heir apparent but 'as an instance of intense grief in the present, he sums up for Vergil and his audience the previous sufferings in Rome's past, and points ahead to sufferings to come'. Caieta, for Segal, is the replacement for the mother who cannot die, and her death completes Aeneas' experience with the loss of persons near and dear to him: Palinurus, Misenus, Anchises and Caieta. He argues against Havet's insertion of 616-20 between 601 and 602 and against the substitution of quo for quo s at v. 602. De Grummond (24) finds a fully realised figure of epic scope in Diomedes, a fighter become philosopher-king, idealized yet warm, dignified, and courageous. A new Diomedes has risen from the old likeness, regenerated in his cherished Saturnia regna . Cannon (25) examines the characterization of Turnus, Pallas, Evander, Venus and Vulcan, and finds them masterly and realistic. He argues that the story is neither an independent, unit nor digression. He regards the story as preparation for the poet's treatment of character and event in 9-12, and as of the utmost importance in foreshadowing the fate of Turnus. raw_text:
Wilhelmina F. Jashemski, 'The Caupona of Euxinus at Pompeii',
Archaeology 20 (1967) 36-44.
Courtney (34) attacks some textual cruces in the Culex , Copa ,
Elegiae in Maecenatem, Ciris, Catalepton (1, and 14), and Moretum .
Colin Hardie's Oxford Text (35) has an excellent introduction to
the Lives. Akbar Khan (36) argues that Horace in Odes /, 24 refers to an elegy which Vergil had written on the death of Quintilius, an elegy which was careless of moral propriety. Horace's rejoinder is a cautionary poem, with a lesson by example, arguing
that even in the case of an inestimable friend's death, pudor and
modus are the proper principles to observe. Wilhelmina Jashemsky's
article (37) offers valuable illustration for the Copa in the shape
of a Pompei an tavern and garden.

Beye's study of the epic tradition (38) treats the Aeneid with
fresh insight and assigns Vergil a position of prominence and
novelty in the tradition. Steele Commager's collected essays (39)
include Bruno Snell's 'The Discovery of a Spiritual Landscape'
14-27, Jacques Perret's 'The Georgios* 28-40, Wendell Clausen's
'An Interpretation of the Aeneid' 75-88; Adam Parry's 'The Two
Voices of Vergil's Aeneid 9 107-123, Bernard Knox's influential
'The Serpent and the Flame: The Imagery of the Second Book of
the Aeneid ' 124-142, and Viktor Poschl's 'Basic Themes', 164-

raw_text:
The collection should be on every teacher's shelf and in
every student's library. Conway's (40) Harvard lectures with its
valuable essays on the Philosophy of Vergil and the Architecture
of the Epic are reprinted for an audience which has neglected them
with serious consequences. Cook's study (41) and Cupaiuolo's
inquiry into the cultural aspects of Latin poetry in the Augustan
Age (42) are both deserving of study and notice. Distler's (43)
handbook for students approaching Vergil and the Aeneid for the
first time offers useful background information on the man and his
times, and his achievement. Jackson Knight's revised Roman

Hall's article (53) examines Scarron's burlesque of Vergil's
Aeneid in 1648-59. He regards Scarron's version as an elaboration
of the 4causerie en vers', a negative guide to the levels of the
sublime. The prelude offers a foretaste of the burlesque which
follows:
Je chante cet homme pieux
Qui vint charge de tous ses Dieux,
Et de Monsieur son pere Anchise,
Beau vieillard a la barbe grise.
Of Deiopeia, the nymph with whom Juno bribes Aeolus in A. i, 4un
vrai visage de poupée', Scarron writes:
Elle entend, et parle fort bied
L'espagnol and l'italien.
A. G. McKay
McMaster University
September, 1967
